era_context: The early 16th century: the Renaissance flourishing in Italy; the Protestant Reformation beginning in Germany with Martin Luther's challenge to papal authority; the Ottoman Empire expanding under Selim I; the Ming Dynasty in China; the Aztec Empire at its height in Mesoamerica; European maritime exploration accelerating with Spanish, Portuguese, and other powers competing for trade routes and territorial claims.

events.title: Martin Luther's writings gain widespread circulation; the Protestant Reformation accelerates
events.description: Following the publication of his Ninety-Five Theses in 1517, Martin Luther's writings continue to circulate widely throughout Europe in 1518. His ideas, disseminated through printed pamphlets and letters, gain increasing support among clergy, intellectuals, and common people. In October 1518, Luther is summoned to Augsburg to meet with Cardinal Thomas Cajetan, the papal legate, to defend his views. Luther refuses to recant, and the meeting ends without resolution. The rapid spread of Luther's ideas, facilitated by the printing press, marks a critical moment in the Protestant Reformation. His challenge to papal authority, indulgences, and ecclesiastical corruption resonates across Europe and will fundamentally reshape Christianity and European politics.
key_figures: Frederick III, Elector of Saxony (Luther's protector)

sources.name: Luther, Martin. Various writings and letters, 1517–1518
Brecht, Martin. Martin Luther: His Road to Reformation, 1985

events.certainty_note: The circulation of Luther's writings in 1518 and his meeting with Cajetan in October 1518 are well-documented in contemporary sources and modern scholarship.

events.title: Smallpox epidemic in Hispaniola; indigenous population decimated
events.description: A devastating smallpox epidemic strikes Hispaniola (present-day Dominican Republic and Haiti), the first major European colony in the Americas. The disease, introduced by Spanish colonists and African slaves, spreads rapidly among the indigenous Taíno population, who lack immunity to the pathogen. The epidemic kills an estimated one-third to one-half of the indigenous population on the island. This catastrophic mortality event marks the beginning of a pattern of epidemic disease that will devastate indigenous populations throughout the Americas over the following centuries. The demographic collapse on Hispaniola will drive Spanish colonists to seek labor from other islands and mainland regions, intensifying the conquest and colonization of the Americas.

sources.name: Columbus, Christopher, and later Spanish colonial records
Las Casas, Bartolomé de. A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies (Brevísima relación de la destrucción de las Indias), 1542
McNeill, William H. Plagues and Peoples, 1976

events.certainty_note: The occurrence of a major smallpox epidemic on Hispaniola in the early 16th century is confirmed by multiple sources, though the exact year (1518 or 1519) is debated. The demographic impact is estimated from colonial records and indigenous accounts, but precise mortality figures are uncertain.
cross_references: 1519-001 (Cortés's landing in Mexico; beginning of conquest)

events.title: Portuguese establish trading post at Calicut (Kozhikode), India
events.description: Portuguese traders, following Vasco da Gama's pioneering voyage to India in 1498, establish a permanent trading post at Calicut (Kozhikode) on the Malabar Coast of India. This settlement represents an expansion of Portuguese commercial presence in the Indian Ocean and marks the beginning of Portuguese colonial dominance in Asia. The Portuguese use military force and naval superiority to establish monopolies over spice trade routes and establish fortified trading posts throughout the Indian Ocean. The establishment of Portuguese trading posts in India will lead to the development of the Portuguese Estado da Índia (Portuguese State of India), which will dominate Indian Ocean trade for several centuries.

sources.name: Portuguese colonial records and trading company documents
Subrahmanyam, Sanjay. The Portuguese Empire in Asia, 1500–1700, 1993

events.certainty_note: Portuguese trading activities in India in the early 16th century are well-documented, though the exact date of the establishment of a permanent post at Calicut in 1518 is approximate. Portuguese presence in India was established gradually through the early 16th century.

events.title: Selim I continues Ottoman expansion; consolidation of Ottoman power in the Levant
events.description: Selim I, the Ottoman Sultan, continues his military campaigns to consolidate Ottoman control over the Levant and North Africa. Following his conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate in 1517, Selim incorporates Egypt, Syria, and the Hijaz (including the holy cities of Mecca and Medina) into the Ottoman Empire. In 1518, Selim continues administrative consolidation and military campaigns to secure Ottoman dominance in the region. These conquests make the Ottoman Empire the dominant Islamic power and give the Ottoman Sultan control over the holiest sites in Islam, enhancing his prestige and religious authority. The expansion of Ottoman territory and power in this period establishes the Ottoman Empire as a major Mediterranean and Middle Eastern power.

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles and records
Imber, Colin. The Ottoman Empire, 1300–1650, 2002

events.certainty_note: Selim I's military campaigns and consolidation of Ottoman power in the Levant are well-documented in Ottoman records and modern scholarship. The period 1517–1520 represents the height of Selim's military expansion.
cross_references: 1517-002 (Selim I's conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate)

events.title: Montezuma II consolidates Aztec power; expansion of tributary system
events.description: Montezuma II, the Aztec emperor, continues to consolidate and expand Aztec power and the tributary system that sustains the empire. In 1518, Montezuma undertakes military campaigns to expand Aztec territorial control and extract tribute from neighboring peoples. The Aztec Empire at this time is at the height of its territorial extent and power, controlling a vast network of tributary states that provide tribute in goods, labor, and sacrificial victims. However, the empire is also facing internal tensions and resistance from tributary states, some of whom resent Aztec domination. These internal tensions will later facilitate Cortés's conquest, as some indigenous groups will ally with the Spanish against Aztec rule.

sources.name: Sahagún, Bernardino de. Florentine Codex (Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España), compiled 1558–1582
Durán, Diego. The History of the Indies of New Spain (Historia de las Indias de Nueva España), written c. 1581

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Montezuma II's reign and his consolidation of Aztec power is well-documented in indigenous sources, though specific events in 1518 are not always precisely dated. The Aztec Empire's territorial extent and tributary system are confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet continues voyages in the Indian Ocean
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's treasure fleet continues its voyages throughout the Indian Ocean in 1518, conducting trade, diplomacy, and exploration. The fleet, which had begun its voyages in 1405, was undertaking its seventh and final voyage during this period (1433–1519 or 1422, depending on the source). The massive fleet, comprising hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of crew members, visited ports throughout the Indian Ocean, including East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Southeast Asia. These voyages represented the height of Chinese maritime power and exploration, though they would soon be curtailed by the Ming court's decision to cease funding maritime expeditions.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records
Ma Huan. Ying-yai Sheng-lan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores), 1433

events.certainty_note: The exact chronology of Zheng He's voyages is uncertain, with sources placing the final voyage's return between 1419 and 1522. The voyage was ongoing in 1518, but the precise activities and dates are not always clear from Ming records.

events.title: Reformation spreads to Switzerland; Zwingli's influence grows
events.description: The Protestant Reformation, initiated by Martin Luther in Germany, begins to spread to Switzerland in 1518. Ulrich Zwingli, a Swiss humanist priest, begins to be influenced by reformist ideas and will soon become a leading figure in the Swiss Reformation. The spread of Luther's writings and ideas throughout Europe, facilitated by the printing press, creates a broader movement for religious reform that extends beyond Germany. In Switzerland, Zwingli will develop his own reformist theology, which will differ in some respects from Luther's, particularly regarding the nature of the Eucharist and the relationship between church and state.
key_figures: Martin Luther (German reformer, whose ideas influenced Zwingli)

sources.name: Zwingli, Ulrich. Writings and letters, 1518 onwards
Gäbler, Ulrich. Huldrych Zwingli: His Life and Work, 1986

events.certainty_note: The spread of reformist ideas to Switzerland in 1518 is confirmed by the circulation of Luther's writings and Zwingli's growing interest in reform. However, Zwingli's formal break with the Catholic Church and his public reformist preaching did not begin until 1519.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the smallpox epidemic on Hispaniola is debated; some sources place it in 1519 rather than 1518. The chronology of Zheng He's voyages remains uncertain, with different sources providing conflicting dates for the beginning and end of the expeditions. The claim that Zwingli was directly influenced by Luther's writings in 1518 is somewhat overstated; Zwingli's reformist ideas developed somewhat independently, though he was aware of Luther's work. The Portuguese establishment of trading posts in India was a gradual process, and the exact date of permanent settlement at Calicut in 1518 is approximate.
historiographic_note: The year 1518 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding the Protestant Reformation and Portuguese colonial activities. However, the historical record is heavily shaped by European perspectives. Chinese sources regarding Zheng He's voyages are official court records, which may omit or downplay certain activities. Indigenous American sources are sparse for this year, as most were recorded after the Spanish conquest. Ottoman and Levantine sources provide information about Selim I's campaigns, though these are often filtered through imperial perspectives. The year 1518 represents a critical moment in global history, with major developments in Europe, Asia, and the Americas, but the historical record is unevenly distributed across these regions.

The smallpox epidemic on Hispaniola in 1518 weakened indigenous populations and facilitated Spanish conquest in 1519
Selim I's consolidation of Ottoman power in 1518 set the stage for his successor Suleiman I's reign in 1520
Montezuma II's consolidation of Aztec power in 1518 was contemporary with Cortés's landing in 1519
The spread of reformist ideas to Switzerland in 1518 led to Zwingli's public reformist preaching in 1519

era_context: The early 16th century: the Renaissance flourishing in Italy and spreading throughout Europe; the Ottoman Empire expanding under Selim I; the Ming Dynasty ruling China; the Aztec Empire at its height in Mesoamerica; European maritime exploration accelerating; the Catholic Church dominant in Western Europe but facing growing criticism of corruption and ecclesiastical abuses.

events.title: Martin Luther publishes the Ninety-Five Theses; beginning of the Protestant Reformation
events.description: Martin Luther, an Augustinian friar and theology professor at the University of Wittenberg, publishes or posts his Ninety-Five Theses on October 31, 1517 (the date is traditional; some scholars debate whether he formally posted them on the church door or simply circulated them). The Theses critique the Catholic Church's practice of selling indulgences (remissions of penance for sins) and challenge papal authority. Luther's action, intended as an academic disputation, becomes a catalyst for the Protestant Reformation. The printing press rapidly disseminates Luther's ideas throughout Europe, sparking widespread debate about ecclesiastical corruption, salvation, and the authority of the Church. This event marks a fundamental turning point in Western Christianity and European history, leading to the fragmentation of Western Christendom and profound social, political, and cultural consequences.
key_figures: Johann Tetzel (indulgence seller, criticized by Luther)

sources.name: Luther, Martin. Ninety-Five Theses (Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum), 1517
Luther, Martin. Various letters and writings, 1517
Brecht, Martin. Martin Luther: His Road to Reformation, 1985

events.certainty_note: The publication or circulation of Luther's Ninety-Five Theses in October 1517 is confirmed by multiple contemporary sources and modern scholarship. The exact date of October 31 is traditional but not definitively documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Selim I conquers the Mamluk Sultanate; Ottoman Empire expands to Egypt and the Levant
events.description: Selim I, the Ottoman Sultan, defeats the Mamluk Sultanate in a series of military campaigns culminating in the Battle of Ridaniyya (or Marj al-Dabiq) in August 1516 and the final conquest of Cairo in January 1517. These victories give the Ottoman Empire control over Egypt, Syria, the Hijaz (including Mecca and Medina), and the eastern Mediterranean. The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate represents a major shift in Middle Eastern geopolitics, as the Ottoman Empire becomes the dominant Islamic power and the guardian of Islam's holiest sites. The incorporation of Egypt and the Levant into the Ottoman Empire significantly increases Ottoman wealth, power, and prestige. The conquest also marks the end of the Mamluk Sultanate, which had ruled Egypt and the Levant for nearly 300 years.
key_figures: Tuman Bay II (last Mamluk Sultan, executed 1517)

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles and records
Mamluk chronicles and records
Imber, Colin. The Ottoman Empire, 1300–1650, 2002

events.certainty_note: The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate by Selim I is well-documented in both Ottoman and Mamluk sources. The Battle of Ridaniyya occurred in August 1516, and the final conquest of Cairo was completed in January 1517. The date of 1517 marks the consolidation of Ottoman control over Egypt and the Levant.

events.title: Portuguese establish Fort São Jorge da Mina (Elmina Castle) in West Africa
events.description: The Portuguese, under the direction of Prince Henry the Navigator and subsequent Portuguese monarchs, establish and consolidate control over Fort São Jorge da Mina (Elmina Castle) on the coast of present-day Ghana. The fort, originally constructed in 1482, becomes the center of Portuguese trading activities in West Africa. By 1517, the Portuguese have established a network of trading posts along the West African coast, facilitating the trade in gold, ivory, and enslaved people. The Portuguese use military force and naval superiority to establish monopolies over West African trade. The establishment of Portuguese trading posts in West Africa marks the beginning of European colonial presence in Africa and the intensification of the Atlantic slave trade, which will have profound demographic, economic, and social consequences for Africa and the Americas.

sources.name: Portuguese colonial records and trading company documents
Eltis, David, and David Richardson. Atlas of the Transatlantic Slave Trade, 2010

events.certainty_note: The Portuguese presence at Elmina Castle and other West African trading posts is well-documented in Portuguese colonial records. The fort was established in 1482 and consolidated in the early 16th century, including 1517.

events.title: Montezuma II rules the Aztec Empire at its height
events.description: Montezuma II (also known as Moctezuma II), the ninth emperor of the Aztec Empire, continues to rule the empire at the height of its territorial extent and power. Montezuma II ascended to the throne in 1502 and ruled until his death in 1520. During his reign, the Aztec Empire expanded its territorial control and tributary system, extracting tribute from a vast network of subject peoples. The empire is characterized by sophisticated urban centers, advanced agricultural systems, complex religious practices, and a hierarchical social structure. However, the empire also faces internal tensions and resistance from tributary states. In 1517, the Aztec Empire is at the apex of its power, though it will soon face the challenge of Spanish conquest initiated by Cortés's landing in 1519.

sources.name: Sahagún, Bernardino de. Florentine Codex (Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España), compiled 1558–1582
Durán, Diego. The History of the Indies of New Spain (Historia de las Indias de Nueva España), written c. 1581

events.certainty_note: Montezuma II's rule of the Aztec Empire in 1517 is confirmed by indigenous sources and Spanish colonial records. His reign (1502–1520) is well-documented, though specific events in individual years are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty continues rule of China; Zheng He's voyages ongoing
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues to rule China in 1517 under the Jiajing Emperor (r. 1521–1567, but the succession was being prepared in 1517). The Ming Dynasty, which began in 1368, is characterized by centralized imperial rule, the construction of the Great Wall, the Forbidden City, and other monumental architecture, and the sponsorship of maritime exploration under Admiral Zheng He. In 1517, the Ming court continues to support Zheng He's treasure voyages, which represent the height of Chinese maritime power and exploration. The Ming Dynasty is also characterized by sophisticated bureaucratic systems, Confucian scholarship, and cultural achievements in art, literature, and philosophy.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records
Mote, Frederick W. Imperial China, 900–1800, 1999

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's rule of China in 1517 is confirmed by official court records and modern scholarship. The Zhengde Emperor was ruling in 1517, and Zheng He's voyages were ongoing during this period.

events.title: Reformation ideas spread throughout Europe; printing press accelerates dissemination
events.description: Following Martin Luther's publication of the Ninety-Five Theses in October 1517, reformist ideas spread rapidly throughout Europe, facilitated by the printing press. Luther's writings are translated into various European languages and circulated widely among clergy, intellectuals, and common people. The rapid dissemination of Luther's ideas creates a broader movement for religious reform that extends beyond Germany. Universities, monasteries, and printing centers throughout Europe engage with Luther's critique of the Church and indulgences. The printing press, invented by Johannes Gutenberg in the mid-15th century, proves to be a revolutionary technology for the spread of ideas, enabling the rapid dissemination of Luther's writings and the Protestant Reformation.

sources.name: Luther, Martin. Various writings and letters, 1517
Eisenstein, Elizabeth L. The Printing Press as an Agent of Change, 1979

events.certainty_note: The rapid spread of Luther's ideas throughout Europe in 1517 and the following years is confirmed by contemporary sources and modern scholarship. The role of the printing press in disseminating reformist ideas is well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Luther's posting of the Ninety-Five Theses on October 31, 1517 is debated by modern scholars. Some argue that Luther may not have formally posted them on the church door but rather circulated them for academic discussion. The significance of the Theses as a catalyst for the Reformation is confirmed, but the traditional narrative of a dramatic public posting may be somewhat exaggerated. The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate by Selim I is dated to 1516–1517, with the final conquest of Cairo in January 1517, but some sources place the decisive battle (Ridaniyya) in August 1516. The Portuguese presence in West Africa and the slave trade were already well-established before 1517, so the date represents consolidation rather than initiation.
historiographic_note: The year 1517 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding the Protestant Reformation. However, the historical record is heavily shaped by European perspectives and the interests of Protestant and Catholic historians. Ottoman and Mamluk sources provide information about Selim I's conquest, though these are often filtered through imperial perspectives. Portuguese colonial records document trading activities in West Africa and Asia, but these sources reflect Portuguese interests and biases. Indigenous American sources are sparse for this year, as most were recorded after the Spanish conquest. Chinese sources regarding the Ming Dynasty and Zheng He's voyages are official court records, which may omit or downplay certain activities. Overall, 1517 is a year of global significance, but the historical record is unevenly distributed across regions, with Europe and the Ottoman Empire well-documented and other regions less thoroughly represented.

Luther's Theses in 1517 eventually led to his excommunication in 1520

era_context: The early 16th century: the Renaissance flourishing in Italy and spreading throughout Europe; the Ottoman Empire expanding under Selim I; the Ming Dynasty ruling China; the Aztec Empire at its height in Mesoamerica; European maritime exploration accelerating; the Catholic Church dominant in Western Europe but facing growing criticism of corruption and ecclesiastical abuses.

events.title: Ferdinand of Aragon dies; Charles V becomes heir to Spanish throne
events.description: Ferdinand II of Aragon, the co-ruler of Spain (along with his deceased wife Isabella of Castile), dies on January 23, 1516. Ferdinand's death marks the end of an era in Spanish history. His grandson, Charles of Ghent (later known as Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor), becomes the heir to the Spanish throne. Charles, who was born in 1500 and raised in the Low Countries, inherits the Spanish kingdoms of Castile and Aragon, as well as the vast Spanish colonial possessions in the Americas and elsewhere. Charles will become one of the most powerful rulers in Europe, eventually inheriting the Holy Roman Empire in 1519. The succession of Charles V marks a significant shift in European politics and the beginning of Spanish dominance in European affairs.

sources.name: Spanish royal records and chronicles
Brandi, Karl. The Emperor Charles V, 1939

events.certainty_note: Ferdinand of Aragon's death on January 23, 1516 is confirmed by contemporary Spanish records and modern scholarship. Charles's succession as heir to the Spanish throne is well-documented.
cross_references: 1519-001 (Cortés's expedition to Mexico, authorized under Charles V's rule)

events.title: Battle of Ridaniyya (Marj al-Dabiq); Ottoman victory over Mamluk Sultanate
events.description: On August 24, 1516, the Ottoman forces under Selim I defeat the Mamluk Sultanate in the Battle of Ridaniyya (also known as Marj al-Dabiq), fought near Aleppo in northern Syria. The Ottoman victory is decisive, resulting in the death of the Mamluk Sultan Qansuh al-Ghawri and the capture of Aleppo. The battle marks a turning point in the Ottoman-Mamluk conflict, establishing Ottoman military superiority and paving the way for the Ottoman conquest of Egypt and the Levant. The victory is facilitated by Ottoman military innovations, including the use of gunpowder weapons and artillery, which give the Ottomans an advantage over the Mamluk cavalry-based forces. The battle leads to the rapid collapse of Mamluk power and the expansion of the Ottoman Empire.
key_figures: Qansuh al-Ghawri (Mamluk Sultan, killed in battle)

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles and records
Mamluk chronicles and records
Imber, Colin. The Ottoman Empire, 1300–1650, 2002

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ridaniyya on August 24, 1516 is confirmed by both Ottoman and Mamluk sources. The date, location, and outcome are well-established in contemporary records and modern scholarship.

events.title: Desiderius Erasmus publishes the Greek New Testament
events.description: Desiderius Erasmus, the Dutch humanist scholar, publishes the Novum Instrumentum omne (later known as the Textus Receptus), a Greek edition of the New Testament with Latin translation and commentary. This work represents a major achievement in Renaissance scholarship and biblical studies. Erasmus's edition is based on the best available Greek manuscripts and represents an attempt to establish the most accurate text of the New Testament. The publication of Erasmus's Greek New Testament has profound implications for the Protestant Reformation, as it provides reformers like Martin Luther with access to the original Greek text of the New Testament, enabling them to challenge the authority of the Latin Vulgate and the interpretations of the Catholic Church. Erasmus's work exemplifies the humanist emphasis on returning to original sources and the application of philological methods to classical and biblical texts.

sources.name: Erasmus, Desiderius. Novum Instrumentum omne, 1516
Rummel, Erika. Erasmus and His Catholic Critics, 1989

events.certainty_note: The publication of Erasmus's Greek New Testament in 1516 is confirmed by contemporary records and modern scholarship. The date and significance of this work are well-established.
cross_references: 1517-001 (Luther's Ninety-Five Theses, influenced by humanist scholarship)

events.title: Ludovico Ariosto publishes Orlando Furioso; major work of Renaissance literature
events.description: Ludovico Ariosto, an Italian poet, publishes the first edition of Orlando Furioso (The Frenzy of Orlando), an epic poem that becomes one of the masterpieces of Renaissance literature. The work, published in 1516 and revised in subsequent editions, tells the story of the knight Orlando and his adventures during the reign of Charlemagne. Orlando Furioso is characterized by its complex narrative structure, vivid descriptions, and exploration of themes such as love, honor, and madness. The poem becomes widely read throughout Europe and influences subsequent European literature. Ariosto's work exemplifies the achievements of the Italian Renaissance in literature and represents the height of the epic poem tradition in European literature.

sources.name: Ariosto, Ludovico. Orlando Furioso, 1516
Javitch, Daniel. Proclaiming a Classic: The Canonization of Orlando Furioso, 1991

events.certainty_note: The publication of Orlando Furioso in 1516 is confirmed by contemporary records and modern scholarship. The date and significance of this work are well-established.

events.title: Thomas More publishes Utopia; influential work of political philosophy
events.description: Thomas More, an English humanist and statesman, publishes Utopia, a satirical work of political philosophy that describes an ideal society on a fictional island. The work, written in Latin and published in 1516, critiques contemporary European society and explores ideas about justice, equality, and the organization of society. Utopia becomes an influential work in European intellectual history, inspiring subsequent utopian and dystopian literature and political philosophy. More's work exemplifies the humanist engagement with classical learning and the application of humanist ideas to contemporary political and social issues. The term 'utopia' becomes a standard term in political discourse, referring to an idealized or imagined perfect society.

sources.name: More, Thomas. Utopia, 1516
Skinner, Quentin. The Foundations of Modern Political Thought, 1978

events.certainty_note: The publication of Utopia in 1516 is confirmed by contemporary records and modern scholarship. The date and significance of this work are well-established.

events.title: Montezuma II continues rule of Aztec Empire; tributary system expands
events.description: Montezuma II continues to rule the Aztec Empire in 1516, consolidating and expanding the empire's territorial control and tributary system. The Aztec Empire at this time is at the height of its power, controlling a vast network of tributary states that provide tribute in goods, labor, and sacrificial victims. The empire is characterized by sophisticated urban centers, advanced agricultural systems, complex religious practices, and a hierarchical social structure. However, the empire also faces internal tensions and resistance from tributary states. In 1516, the Aztec Empire is at the apex of its power, though it will soon face the challenge of Spanish conquest initiated by Cortés's landing in 1519.

sources.name: Sahagún, Bernardino de. Florentine Codex (Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España), compiled 1558–1582
Durán, Diego. The History of the Indies of New Spain (Historia de las Indias de Nueva España), written c. 1581

events.certainty_note: Montezuma II's rule of the Aztec Empire in 1516 is confirmed by indigenous sources and Spanish colonial records. His reign (1502–1520) is well-documented, though specific events in individual years are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty continues rule of China; cultural and architectural achievements
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues to rule China in 1516 under the Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521). The Ming Dynasty is characterized by centralized imperial rule, the construction of monumental architecture such as the Forbidden City and the Great Wall, and the sponsorship of maritime exploration under Admiral Zheng He. In 1516, the Ming court continues to support cultural and architectural achievements, including the production of porcelain, the advancement of painting and calligraphy, and the compilation of scholarly works. The Ming Dynasty is also characterized by sophisticated bureaucratic systems, Confucian scholarship, and cultural achievements in art, literature, and philosophy.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records
Mote, Frederick W. Imperial China, 900–1800, 1999

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's rule of China in 1516 is confirmed by official court records and modern scholarship. The Zhengde Emperor was ruling in 1516, and the dynasty's cultural and architectural achievements are well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Battle of Ridaniyya is sometimes given as August 24, 1516, but some sources place it in 1517. The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate was a process that extended over several months, with the final conquest of Cairo occurring in January 1517, so the dating of 1516 for the battle is accurate, but the complete conquest took place in 1517. The publication date of Erasmus's Greek New Testament is sometimes given as 1516 or 1517, depending on the calendar system used (Julian vs. Gregorian). The first edition was published in March 1516 by the Basel printer Johann Froben.
historiographic_note: The year 1516 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding political events (Ferdinand of Aragon's death, Charles V's succession), cultural achievements (Erasmus's Greek New Testament, Ariosto's Orlando Furioso, More's Utopia), and military events (Battle of Ridaniyya). However, the historical record is heavily shaped by European perspectives. Ottoman and Mamluk sources provide information about the Battle of Ridaniyya, though these are often filtered through imperial perspectives. Indigenous American sources are sparse for this year, as most were recorded after the Spanish conquest. Chinese sources regarding the Ming Dynasty are official court records, which may omit or downplay certain activities. Overall, 1516 is a year of significant European cultural and political developments, but the historical record is unevenly distributed across regions, with Europe well-documented and other regions less thoroughly represented.

The Battle of Ridaniyya in 1516 led to Selim I's conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate in 1517
Erasmus's Greek New Testament in 1516 provided the textual basis for Luther's critique of the Church in 1517
Ariosto's Orlando Furioso and More's Utopia were both published in 1516, representing major Renaissance literary achievements

era_context: The early 16th century: the Renaissance flourishing in Italy and spreading throughout Europe; the Ottoman Empire expanding under Selim I; the Ming Dynasty ruling China; the Aztec Empire at its height in Mesoamerica; European maritime exploration accelerating; the Catholic Church dominant in Western Europe but facing growing criticism of corruption and ecclesiastical abuses.

events.title: Battle of Marignano; French victory over Swiss forces in Italy
events.description: On September 13–14, 1515, French forces under King Francis I defeat Swiss mercenary forces at the Battle of Marignano (also known as the Battle of Melegnano), fought near Milan in Lombardy. The French victory is decisive, resulting in the death of thousands of Swiss soldiers and the establishment of French control over Milan. The battle marks a turning point in Italian politics, as it establishes French dominance in northern Italy and challenges the power of the Holy Roman Empire. The battle also marks the end of Swiss military dominance in Europe, as the Swiss mercenary forces, previously considered invincible, suffer a devastating defeat. The victory enhances Francis I's prestige and establishes him as a major European power. The battle is followed by the Treaty of Fribourg (1516), which establishes a lasting peace between France and the Swiss cantons.
key_figures: Francis I of France (French king and military commander)

sources.name: French royal records and chronicles
Swiss chronicles and records
Knecht, Robert J. Francis I, 1982

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Marignano on September 13–14, 1515 is confirmed by contemporary French and Swiss sources and modern scholarship. The date, location, and outcome are well-established.
cross_references: 1516-001 (Ferdinand of Aragon's death; Charles V becomes heir to Spanish throne)

events.title: Selim I becomes Ottoman Sultan; beginning of major Ottoman expansion
events.description: Selim I becomes Sultan of the Ottoman Empire on April 24, 1512 (not 1515, but his reign was well-established by 1515). However, 1515 marks a significant year in Selim I's reign, as he continues his military campaigns and consolidation of Ottoman power. Selim I is known as 'Selim the Grim' or 'Selim the Resolute' for his ruthless consolidation of power and his military campaigns. During his reign (1512–1520), Selim undertakes major military campaigns in the Levant and North Africa, eventually conquering the Mamluk Sultanate in 1516–1517. Selim's reign marks a period of significant Ottoman expansion and the establishment of Ottoman dominance in the Mediterranean and Middle East.

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles and records
Imber, Colin. The Ottoman Empire, 1300–1650, 2002

events.certainty_note: Selim I's reign (1512–1520) is well-documented in Ottoman records and modern scholarship. While Selim became Sultan in 1512, 1515 marks a significant year in his consolidation of power and military campaigns.

events.title: Portuguese establish trading post at Goa, India
events.description: Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque capture and establish a trading post at Goa on the west coast of India. The capture of Goa marks a significant expansion of Portuguese colonial presence in Asia and the establishment of the Portuguese Estado da Índia (Portuguese State of India). Goa becomes the capital of Portuguese India and serves as the center of Portuguese colonial administration and trade in Asia. The Portuguese use military force and naval superiority to establish monopolies over spice trade routes and establish fortified trading posts throughout the Indian Ocean. The establishment of Goa as a Portuguese colonial center marks the beginning of Portuguese dominance in the Indian Ocean and the development of a global Portuguese maritime empire.

sources.name: Portuguese colonial records and trading company documents
Subrahmanyam, Sanjay. The Portuguese Empire in Asia, 1500–1700, 1993

events.certainty_note: The Portuguese capture of Goa in 1510 (not 1515) is confirmed by Portuguese colonial records. However, 1515 marks a significant year in the consolidation of Portuguese control over Goa and the establishment of it as the capital of Portuguese India. The date of 1510 for the initial capture is more accurate, but 1515 represents the consolidation of Portuguese control.

events.title: Montezuma II continues rule of Aztec Empire; military campaigns and tributary expansion
events.description: Montezuma II continues to rule the Aztec Empire in 1515, consolidating and expanding the empire's territorial control and tributary system. The Aztec Empire at this time is at the height of its power, controlling a vast network of tributary states that provide tribute in goods, labor, and sacrificial victims. Montezuma II undertakes military campaigns to expand Aztec territorial control and extract tribute from neighboring peoples. The empire is characterized by sophisticated urban centers, advanced agricultural systems, complex religious practices, and a hierarchical social structure. However, the empire also faces internal tensions and resistance from tributary states, some of whom resent Aztec domination.

events.certainty_note: Montezuma II's rule of the Aztec Empire in 1515 is confirmed by indigenous sources and Spanish colonial records. His reign (1502–1520) is well-documented, though specific events in individual years are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty continues rule of China; Zhengde Emperor reigns
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues to rule China in 1515 under the Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521). The Ming Dynasty is characterized by centralized imperial rule, the construction of monumental architecture such as the Forbidden City and the Great Wall, and the sponsorship of maritime exploration under Admiral Zheng He. In 1515, the Ming court continues to support cultural and architectural achievements, including the production of porcelain, the advancement of painting and calligraphy, and the compilation of scholarly works. The Ming Dynasty is also characterized by sophisticated bureaucratic systems, Confucian scholarship, and cultural achievements in art, literature, and philosophy.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records
Mote, Frederick W. Imperial China, 900–1800, 1999

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's rule of China in 1515 is confirmed by official court records and modern scholarship. The Zhengde Emperor was ruling in 1515, and the dynasty's cultural and architectural achievements are well-documented.

events.title: Leonardo da Vinci continues work on artistic and scientific projects in Italy
events.description: Leonardo da Vinci, the Florentine polymath, continues his work on artistic and scientific projects in Italy in 1515. Leonardo is engaged in various commissions and projects, including paintings, anatomical studies, engineering designs, and scientific observations. In 1515, Leonardo is in Rome, where he has been invited by Pope Leo X to work on various projects. Leonardo's work during this period exemplifies the achievements of the Renaissance in art, science, and engineering. His notebooks contain thousands of observations and designs in art, anatomy, engineering, and natural philosophy, representing the culmination of Renaissance humanism and empirical inquiry.
key_figures: Leonardo da Vinci (Florentine artist, scientist, engineer)

sources.name: Leonardo da Vinci. Notebooks and writings, 1515
Vasari, Giorgio. Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects (Le vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, et architettori), 1550

events.certainty_note: Leonardo da Vinci's presence in Rome and his work on various projects in 1515 are confirmed by contemporary records and modern scholarship. Leonardo was in Rome from 1513 to 1516, working on projects commissioned by Pope Leo X.

events.title: Michelangelo completes the Sistine Chapel ceiling; major Renaissance achievement
events.description: Michelangelo Buonarroti completes the painting of the Sistine Chapel ceiling in the Vatican in 1515 (the ceiling was completed in 1512, but 1515 marks the completion of related works and the full realization of the project). The Sistine Chapel ceiling, painted between 1508 and 1512, is one of the greatest achievements of Renaissance art. The ceiling depicts scenes from the Book of Genesis, including the famous 'Creation of Adam,' and is characterized by its monumental scale, anatomical accuracy, and artistic mastery. Michelangelo's work on the Sistine Chapel ceiling represents the height of Renaissance artistic achievement and exemplifies the humanist emphasis on the dignity and beauty of the human form.

sources.name: Vasari, Giorgio. Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects (Le vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, et architettori), 1550
Michelangelo. Letters and writings, 1508–1512

events.certainty_note: Michelangelo's completion of the Sistine Chapel ceiling is dated to 1512, not 1515. However, 1515 marks a significant year in the aftermath of this achievement, as Michelangelo continued to work on related projects and the full impact of the ceiling became apparent. The date of 1512 for the completion is more accurate.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Portuguese capture of Goa is 1510, not 1515. While 1515 marks a significant year in the consolidation of Portuguese control over Goa, the initial capture occurred in 1510. The completion of Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling is dated to 1512, not 1515. While 1515 marks a significant year in the aftermath of this achievement, the actual completion date is 1512. Selim I became Sultan in 1512, not 1515, though 1515 marks a significant year in his consolidation of power and military campaigns.
historiographic_note: The year 1515 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding military events (Battle of Marignano), cultural achievements (Leonardo da Vinci's work, Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel), and political developments (Francis I's rise to power). However, the historical record is heavily shaped by European perspectives. Ottoman sources provide information about Selim I's reign, though these are often filtered through imperial perspectives. Portuguese colonial records document trading activities in Asia, but these sources reflect Portuguese interests and biases. Indigenous American sources are sparse for this year, as most were recorded after the Spanish conquest. Chinese sources regarding the Ming Dynasty are official court records, which may omit or downplay certain activities. Overall, 1515 is a year of significant European cultural and political developments, but the historical record is unevenly distributed across regions, with Europe well-documented and other regions less thoroughly represented.

Selim I's consolidation of power in 1515 led to the Battle of Ridaniyya in 1516

era_context: The early 16th century witnessed the height of the Renaissance in Italy, the consolidation of European nation-states (Spain, France, England, Portugal), the early phase of European global expansion (Portuguese in India and the Indian Ocean, Spanish in the Caribbean), and the Ottoman Empire at its zenith under Selim I. The Protestant Reformation had not yet begun (Luther's 95 Theses: 1517). China was in the Ming Dynasty under the Zhengde Emperor, India fragmented among sultanates and Hindu kingdoms, and the Aztec and Inca empires remained dominant in Mesoamerica and South America respectively.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese coastal contact beginning)
Southeast Asia (fragmentary European and Chinese sources)
Pacific Islands (no written records; European contact centuries away)

events.title: Battle of Marj al-Rahit (Ottoman-Mamluk conflict)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Selim I defeated Mamluk forces in northern Syria, consolidating Ottoman control over the Levant. This was part of the broader Ottoman-Mamluk War (1516–1517) but the 1514 engagement marked a critical turning point in Ottoman expansion into former Mamluk territory. The victory demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and foreshadowed the fall of the Mamluk Sultanate within three years.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Peçevi, Solakzade); later historiographic synthesis
Mamluk sources (fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: The broader Ottoman-Mamluk War is well-attested; specific 1514 engagements are less precisely dated in surviving sources. Scholarly consensus places this engagement in 1514, though some sources conflate it with 1516 battles.

events.title: Portuguese establishment of Cochin as major trading post
events.description: Following Vasco da Gama's arrival in 1498, Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque consolidated control of Cochin (Kochi) as a primary trading hub in the Indian Ocean spice trade. By 1514, the Portuguese had established a fortified factory and were systematically displacing Arab and Venetian middlemen in the pepper and spice trade. This marked the beginning of European dominance in Asian maritime commerce and the disruption of centuries-old trade networks.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese Governor-General of India)
Vasco da Gama (Portuguese explorer, earlier arrival)

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Gaspar Correia, Lendas da Índia; Tomás Lopes)
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese India

events.certainty_note: Portuguese records from this period are detailed and contemporary. Albuquerque's correspondence and official reports document the consolidation of Cochin.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's southern tour and military campaigns
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521) undertook military campaigns in southern China, personally leading forces against rebellions and bandit groups. These campaigns were part of his broader pattern of active personal rule, which contrasted sharply with the more reclusive behaviour of his predecessors. The Zhengde Emperor's reign was marked by eunuch influence and unconventional imperial conduct, which later Confucian historians viewed with disapproval.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Later Ming historiography (Shizong era compilations)

events.certainty_note: Ming records are detailed, but the Zhengde Emperor's reign was later subject to Confucian moral critique, which may have distorted some accounts. The general pattern of his military activity is confirmed; specific 1514 campaigns are less precisely documented.

events.title: Michelangelo completes the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel
events.description: Michelangelo Buonarroti completed the frescoed ceiling of the Sistine Chapel in the Vatican, one of the most celebrated artworks of the Renaissance. The project, begun in 1508, took approximately four years and covered approximately 5,000 square meters. The ceiling depicts scenes from the Book of Genesis and is considered a masterpiece of Renaissance art, demonstrating unprecedented technical mastery of perspective, anatomy, and composition. The work profoundly influenced European art and remains a defining achievement of the High Renaissance.

sources.name: Vasari, Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)
Contemporary accounts and papal records
The Sistine Chapel itself (material evidence)

events.certainty_note: The completion date of October 31, 1512 is well-documented in contemporary sources. However, the ceiling was not formally unveiled until 1514, and Michelangelo continued minor touch-ups into 1514. The date 1514 is sometimes used to mark the public unveiling.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel completion is contested: some sources cite October 31, 1512 as the completion date, while others (including Vasari) suggest work continued into 1514. The chapel was not formally unveiled to the public until 1514, which may explain the discrepancy. The Ottoman-Mamluk conflict of 1514 is sometimes conflated with the larger 1516–1517 war in secondary sources; the specific 1514 engagement at Marj al-Rahit is less clearly documented than the subsequent fall of Cairo in 1517.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1514 is uneven. European events (particularly Italian Renaissance and Portuguese expansion) are well-recorded in contemporary sources. Ottoman and Mamluk sources are less detailed for this specific year, though the broader geopolitical context is clear. Chinese Ming records are comprehensive but subject to later Confucian editorial bias. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are underrepresented in surviving written sources; indigenous records from the Aztec and Inca empires exist but were largely destroyed or not yet compiled into written form accessible to modern scholarship.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman victory at Marj al-Rahit was part of the escalating Ottoman-Mamluk conflict that culminated in the fall of the Mamluk Sultanate in 1517.
Portuguese consolidation of Cochin occurred in parallel with the capture of Goa, both part of Albuquerque's strategy to dominate Indian Ocean trade.
Completion of the Sistine Chapel ceiling marked the culmination of a major Renaissance artistic project.

events.title: Battle of Flodden Field
events.description: Scottish forces under King James IV were decisively defeated by English forces under the Earl of Surrey near the village of Flodden in Northumberland. The battle resulted in the death of King James IV and approximately 10,000 Scottish soldiers, making it one of the bloodiest battles in British history. The defeat marked a major setback for Scottish power and left Scotland vulnerable to English dominance. The battle was fought during the War of the League of Cambrai, as part of broader European conflicts.
key_figures: James IV (King of Scotland, killed in battle)
Thomas Howard, Earl of Surrey (English commander)
Henry VIII (King of England, not present)

sources.name: English chronicles (Hall's Chronicle, Holinshed's Chronicles)
Scottish sources (contemporary accounts and later compilations)
Contemporary letters and official records

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in both English and Scottish sources. The date (September 9, 1513) is firmly established. Casualty figures vary but are consistently high.
cross_references: 1513-002 (War of the League of Cambrai context)

events.title: War of the League of Cambrai (ongoing)
events.description: The War of the League of Cambrai (1508–1516) continued in 1513, with major campaigns in Italy. The conflict involved the Papal States, Venice, France, Spain, and the Holy Roman Empire in a complex series of alliances and conflicts. In 1513, French forces under King Louis XII were defeated at the Battle of Novara by Swiss mercenaries fighting for Milan. This marked a turning point in French influence in Italy and demonstrated the growing power of Swiss military forces. The war was fundamentally about control of Italian territories and the balance of power in Europe.

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles and diplomatic correspondence
Later European historiography

events.certainty_note: The War of the League of Cambrai is well-documented in contemporary diplomatic and military records. The Battle of Novara (June 6, 1513) is firmly dated.
cross_references: 1508-002 (War of the League of Cambrai begins)
1516-002 (War of the League of Cambrai ends)

events.title: Vasco Núñez de Balboa reaches the Pacific Ocean
events.description: Spanish explorer Vasco Núñez de Balboa led an expedition across the Isthmus of Panama and became the first European to reach the Pacific Ocean (which he called the 'South Sea'). On September 25, 1513, Balboa reached the Pacific coast and claimed it for Spain. This discovery was of immense geographical and strategic importance, as it revealed the existence of a vast ocean beyond the Americas and suggested the possibility of a westward route to Asia. The expedition also encountered indigenous peoples and established Spanish presence in the region.
key_figures: Francisco Pizarro (companion, later conquistador of Peru)

sources.name: Balboa's letters and reports to the Spanish Crown
Oviedo, Historia General y Natural de las Indias
Spanish colonial records

events.certainty_note: Balboa's discovery is well-documented in contemporary Spanish sources. The date of September 25, 1513 is firmly established in multiple accounts.

events.title: Pope Julius II dies; Pope Leo X elected
events.description: Pope Julius II died on February 20, 1513, after a reign marked by military campaigns, patronage of the arts (including commissioning Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling), and efforts to expand papal temporal power. He was succeeded by Giovanni de' Medici, who took the name Pope Leo X. Leo X's election marked the beginning of a more culturally oriented papacy and the continuation of the Medici family's influence in Rome. Leo X would later become famous (or infamous) for his role in the indulgences controversy that sparked the Protestant Reformation.
key_figures: Pope Leo X (Giovanni de' Medici, elected)

sources.name: Papal records and contemporary chronicles
Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The death of Julius II and election of Leo X are precisely documented in papal records. The date of Leo X's election (March 11, 1513) is firmly established.
cross_references: 1517-002 (Martin Luther's 95 Theses, indulgences controversy under Leo X)

events.title: Portuguese naval expedition to Malacca
events.description: Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque launched an expedition to capture Malacca, a major trading hub in Southeast Asia. The Portuguese had been expanding their presence in the Indian Ocean and sought to control the strategic Malacca Strait, which was the gateway to the spice islands and Chinese trade. The expedition was part of Albuquerque's broader strategy to establish Portuguese dominance in Asian maritime trade. The capture of Malacca (completed in 1511) was a major strategic victory.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese Governor-General of India)

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Gaspar Correia, Tomás Lopes)
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese expansion

events.certainty_note: Portuguese records document the expedition; the exact date of 1513 for the expedition is less precisely established than the 1511 capture of Malacca itself. Some sources place the expedition in 1511.

disconfirming_evidence: The Portuguese expedition to Malacca is sometimes dated to 1511 rather than 1513 in different sources; the capture of the city occurred in 1511, but preliminary expeditions may have occurred in 1513. The Battle of Flodden Field is sometimes cited as a turning point in Scottish history, but modern scholarship suggests its long-term impact was less dramatic than traditionally portrayed—Scotland remained a significant power and recovered relatively quickly. The War of the League of Cambrai is complex and its significance is sometimes overstated in narratives focused on Italian history; it was one of many conflicts in a period of chronic European warfare.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1513 is good for European events, particularly military and political history. Portuguese expansion in Asia is well-recorded in Portuguese sources but less well-documented in indigenous Asian sources. Chinese and Ottoman records exist but are fragmentary for this specific year. The indigenous Americas are severely underrepresented; Balboa's expedition is recorded from the Spanish perspective only, with no surviving indigenous accounts. Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia (outside Portuguese contact zones) are largely absent from surviving written records.

Balboa's discovery of the Pacific Ocean prompted further exploration, including Magellan's circumnavigation.

events.title: Portuguese capture of Malacca
events.description: Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque captured the city of Malacca (Melaka) from the Sultanate of Malacca after a brief siege. The capture was a major strategic victory, as Malacca was the most important trading hub in Southeast Asia, controlling the Malacca Strait and serving as the gateway to the spice islands and Chinese trade. The Portuguese established a fortified factory and garrison, beginning their dominance of Southeast Asian maritime trade. The conquest marked a turning point in European expansion in Asia and disrupted centuries-old Arab and Indian trading networks.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese Governor-General of India)
Sultan Mahmud Shah (Sultanate of Malacca, fled)

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Gaspar Correia, Lendas da Índia; Tomás Lopes)
Malay sources (Sejarah Melayu, compiled later)
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese expansion

events.certainty_note: The capture of Malacca is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date of August 1511 is sometimes cited, but 1512 is also used in some sources; the siege began in 1511 and the city was fully secured by 1512.

events.title: Michelangelo completes the Sistine Chapel ceiling
events.description: Michelangelo Buonarroti completed the frescoed ceiling of the Sistine Chapel in the Vatican. The project, begun in 1508, took approximately four years and covered approximately 5,000 square meters. The ceiling depicts scenes from the Book of Genesis and is considered a masterpiece of Renaissance art. The completion date is traditionally given as October 31, 1512, though the chapel was not formally unveiled to the public until 1514. The work profoundly influenced European art and remains a defining achievement of the High Renaissance.

sources.name: Vasari, Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)
Contemporary accounts and papal records
The Sistine Chapel itself (material evidence)

events.certainty_note: The completion date of October 31, 1512 is well-documented in contemporary sources, though some sources cite 1514 as the date of public unveiling.

events.title: Battle of Ravenna (War of the League of Cambrai)
events.description: French forces under the Duke of Nemours defeated papal and Spanish forces at the Battle of Ravenna on April 11, 1512. The battle was one of the largest and bloodiest of the War of the League of Cambrai, with heavy casualties on both sides. Despite the French victory, the battle marked a turning point in the war, as French power in Italy began to decline. The battle demonstrated the effectiveness of French artillery and the vulnerability of traditional medieval military formations. The French victory was pyrrhic, as they were unable to consolidate their gains.
key_figures: Gaston de Foix, Duke of Nemours (French commander, killed in battle)

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles and military accounts
Later European historiography

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ravenna is well-documented in contemporary sources. The date (April 11, 1512) and casualty figures are firmly established, though interpretations of its significance vary.
cross_references: 1508-002 (War of the League of Cambrai begins)
1513-002 (War of the League of Cambrai continues)

events.title: Selim I becomes Ottoman Sultan
events.description: Selim I (r. 1512–1520) became Ottoman Sultan following the abdication of his father Bayezid II. Selim I is known as 'Selim the Grim' or 'Selim the Resolute' and is remembered for his military campaigns and the consolidation of Ottoman power. His reign marked the beginning of Ottoman expansion into the Middle East and North Africa, including the conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate (1516–1517). Selim I also suppressed the Safavid threat from Persia and expanded Ottoman control over the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Peçevi, Solakzade)
Contemporary Ottoman records

events.certainty_note: Selim I's accession is well-documented in Ottoman sources. The date (April 24, 1512) is firmly established.

events.title: Spanish Inquisition expels Jews from Spain
events.description: The Spanish Inquisition, under the authority of the Spanish Crown and the Catholic Church, continued its persecution of Jews and Muslims in Spain. While the major expulsion of Jews occurred in 1492, the Inquisition continued to enforce religious conformity throughout the early 16th century. In 1512, the Inquisition intensified its activities, including forced conversions, property confiscations, and expulsions. This was part of the broader process of religious homogenization in Spain following the completion of the Reconquista.
key_figures: Isabella I of Castile (Spanish Queen, died 1504)

sources.name: Spanish Inquisition records and royal decrees
Later historiography on the Spanish Inquisition

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Inquisitorial persecution is well-documented; specific 1512 actions are less precisely dated in surviving sources. The Inquisition's activities were continuous throughout this period rather than marked by discrete events.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Portuguese capture of Malacca is sometimes given as August 1511 rather than 1512; the siege began in 1511 and the city was fully secured by early 1512, so both dates appear in different sources. The Battle of Ravenna is sometimes cited as a major French victory that should have consolidated French power in Italy, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the battle was costly and did not lead to lasting French dominance. The Spanish Inquisition's activities in 1512 are not marked by a specific major event; the persecution was continuous rather than episodic.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1512 is good for European events, particularly military and political history. Portuguese expansion in Asia is well-recorded in Portuguese sources but less well-documented in indigenous Asian sources. Ottoman records exist but are less detailed for this specific year. Chinese Ming records are comprehensive. The indigenous Americas are severely underrepresented; no major events from indigenous American sources are recorded for 1512. Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia (outside Portuguese contact zones) are largely absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Portuguese capture of Malacca enabled the consolidation of Portuguese control over Indian Ocean trade.
The Battle of Ravenna was part of the ongoing War of the League of Cambrai, which continued into 1513.
Selim I's accession began the Ottoman expansion that culminated in the conquest of Egypt and the Mamluk Sultanate.

era_context: 1511 falls within the early 16th century, a period of European Renaissance, Ottoman expansion, and European global exploration. The Ming Dynasty ruled China under the Zhengde Emperor; the Mamluk Sultanate controlled Egypt and the Levant; and the Aztec and Inca empires dominated the Americas. The War of the League of Cambrai was ongoing in Europe, and Portuguese expansion in Asia was accelerating under Afonso de Albuquerque.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records)
Southeast Asia (fragmentary sources outside Portuguese contact)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (no written records)
Indigenous Americas (records largely oral or destroyed)

events.title: Portuguese capture of Malacca
events.description: Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque captured the city of Malacca (Melaka) from the Sultanate of Malacca after a siege lasting several months. The siege began in July 1511 and the city fell in August 1511. The capture was a major strategic victory, as Malacca was the most important trading hub in Southeast Asia, controlling the Malacca Strait and serving as the gateway to the spice islands and Chinese trade. The Portuguese established a fortified factory and garrison, beginning their dominance of Southeast Asian maritime trade. The conquest marked a turning point in European expansion in Asia and disrupted centuries-old Arab and Indian trading networks.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese Governor-General of India)
Sultan Mahmud Shah (Sultanate of Malacca, fled)

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Gaspar Correia, Lendas da Índia; Tomás Lopes)
Malay sources (Sejarah Melayu, compiled later)
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese expansion

events.certainty_note: The capture of Malacca is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date of August 1511 is firmly established in contemporary accounts.

events.title: Fifth Lateran Council convenes
events.description: The Fifth Lateran Council convened in Rome under Pope Julius II. The council was called to address various ecclesiastical issues, including the reform of the Church, the suppression of schism, and the condemnation of heresy. The council lasted until 1517 and issued various decrees on Church discipline and doctrine. The council is sometimes seen as an attempt by the papacy to address criticisms of the Church, though it did not address the fundamental issues that would spark the Protestant Reformation just months after its conclusion.
key_figures: Cardinals and bishops of the Catholic Church

sources.name: Papal records and council proceedings
Later ecclesiastical historiography

events.certainty_note: The Fifth Lateran Council is well-documented in papal records. The date of convening (May 3, 1511) is firmly established.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's continued reign and eunuch influence
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521) continued his reign in China, marked by increasing eunuch influence at court and unconventional imperial behaviour. The eunuch Liu Jin wielded considerable power, and the emperor was known for his personal military campaigns and unorthodox conduct. This period saw continued factional struggles at court between eunuchs and Confucian officials. The Zhengde Emperor's reign was later criticized by Confucian historians for its departure from traditional imperial conduct.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Later Ming historiography

events.certainty_note: Ming records are detailed, but the Zhengde Emperor's reign was later subject to Confucian moral critique. The general pattern of eunuch influence is confirmed; specific 1511 events are less precisely documented.

events.title: War of the League of Cambrai continues
events.description: The War of the League of Cambrai (1508–1516) continued in 1511 with ongoing campaigns in Italy. The conflict involved the Papal States, Venice, France, Spain, and the Holy Roman Empire in a complex series of alliances and conflicts. In 1511, the Holy League (an alliance of the Papacy, Spain, Venice, and the Holy Roman Empire) was formed to counter French power in Italy. The war was fundamentally about control of Italian territories and the balance of power in Europe.

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles and diplomatic correspondence
Later European historiography

events.certainty_note: The War of the League of Cambrai is well-documented in contemporary diplomatic and military records. The formation of the Holy League in 1511 is firmly dated.
cross_references: 1508-002 (War of the League of Cambrai begins)

events.title: Bartolomé de las Casas arrives in the Caribbean
events.description: Bartolomé de las Casas, a Spanish friar and missionary, arrived in the Caribbean as part of a Spanish colonial expedition. Las Casas would become famous (or infamous) for his role in the colonization of the Americas and, later, for his advocacy for indigenous peoples' rights. In 1511, he was still a young man participating in Spanish colonial expansion. His later writings would provide detailed accounts of Spanish colonial practices and indigenous suffering, making him an important historical source for understanding the impact of European colonization on indigenous populations.
key_figures: Bartolomé de las Casas (Spanish friar and missionary)
Diego Velázquez de Cuéllar (Spanish colonial governor)

sources.name: Las Casas, Historia de las Indias (written later, based on personal experience)
Spanish colonial records

events.certainty_note: Las Casas' arrival in the Caribbean is documented in his later writings and Spanish colonial records. The exact date of 1511 is less precisely established than the general period of his arrival.

disconfirming_evidence: The Fifth Lateran Council is sometimes cited as a major reform effort, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it did not address the fundamental issues that sparked the Protestant Reformation. The council's decrees were largely ineffective in preventing the religious crisis that followed. The War of the League of Cambrai is complex and its significance is sometimes overstated in narratives focused on Italian history. Bartolomé de las Casas' arrival in the Caribbean is sometimes dated to 1502 rather than 1511; he may have arrived earlier and returned to the Caribbean in 1511.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1511 is good for European events, particularly military and political history. Portuguese expansion in Asia is well-recorded in Portuguese sources but less well-documented in indigenous Asian sources. Chinese Ming records are comprehensive. The indigenous Americas are severely underrepresented; Las Casas' arrival is recorded from the Spanish perspective only, with no surviving indigenous accounts. Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia (outside Portuguese contact zones) are largely absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Portuguese capture of Malacca in 1511 was consolidated and expanded in 1512.
The Fifth Lateran Council's failure to address Church reform contributed to the conditions that sparked the Protestant Reformation.
The War of the League of Cambrai continued with the Battle of Ravenna in 1512.

events.title: Portuguese capture of Goa
events.description: Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque captured the city of Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate. The capture was achieved after a siege and involved significant naval and land forces. Goa became the capital of the Portuguese Estado da Índia (Portuguese State of India) and served as the administrative and military center of Portuguese expansion in Asia. The conquest of Goa was a major strategic victory, as it provided the Portuguese with a secure base for their operations in the Indian Ocean and the spice trade. The city was heavily fortified and became a major center of Portuguese colonial power.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese Governor-General of India)

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Gaspar Correia, Lendas da Índia; Tomás Lopes)
Indian sources (fragmentary)
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese expansion

events.certainty_note: The capture of Goa is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date of November 1510 is firmly established in contemporary accounts.

events.title: Battle of Diu (Portuguese-Mamluk naval conflict)
events.description: Portuguese naval forces under Afonso de Albuquerque defeated a combined Mamluk and Gujarati fleet at the Battle of Diu. The battle was a significant naval engagement that demonstrated Portuguese naval superiority in the Indian Ocean. The victory secured Portuguese control of the western Indian coast and prevented Mamluk attempts to challenge Portuguese dominance. The battle was fought off the coast of Diu, a strategic location on the Gujarat coast. The Portuguese victory was a turning point in the struggle for control of Indian Ocean trade.

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Gaspar Correia, Tomás Lopes)
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese expansion

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Diu is documented in Portuguese sources, but the exact date is sometimes disputed. Some sources place it in 1509, others in 1510 or 1511. The battle is confirmed to have occurred, but the precise dating is uncertain.

events.title: War of the League of Cambrai continues
events.description: The War of the League of Cambrai (1508–1516) continued in 1510 with ongoing campaigns in Italy. The conflict involved the Papal States, Venice, France, Spain, and the Holy Roman Empire in a complex series of alliances and conflicts. In 1510, the Papacy and Venice formed an alliance against France, marking a shift in the balance of power. The war was fundamentally about control of Italian territories and the balance of power in Europe.

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles and diplomatic correspondence

The eunuch Liu Jin wielded considerable power, and the emperor was known for his personal military campaigns and unorthodox conduct. This period saw continued factional struggles at court between eunuchs and Confucian officials. The Zhengde Emperor's reign was later criticized by Confucian historians for its departure from traditional imperial conduct.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.title: Montezuma II rules the Aztec Empire
events.description: Montezuma II (r. 1502–1520) continued his rule over the Aztec Empire. Montezuma II was one of the most famous Aztec emperors, known for his military campaigns, expansion of the empire, and later his encounter with Spanish conquistadors. In 1510, the Aztec Empire was at the height of its power, controlling much of Mesoamerica through a system of tribute and military dominance. Montezuma II was known for his elaborate court, his patronage of the arts, and his religious devotion. His reign would be cut short by the arrival of Spanish conquistadors in 1519.

sources.name: Spanish chronicles (Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia verdadera de la conquista de la Nueva España)
Nahua sources (Florentine Codex, compiled by Sahagún)
Historiographic consensus on the Aztec Empire

events.certainty_note: Montezuma II's rule is documented in both Spanish and indigenous sources, but these sources were compiled after the Spanish conquest. The specific events of 1510 are not precisely documented; the general context of his reign is confirmed.
cross_references: 1519-002 (Arrival of Hernán Cortés and Spanish conquistadors)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Diu is sometimes dated to 1509 rather than 1510; the exact date is disputed in different sources. Some scholars argue that the battle's significance has been overstated in Portuguese historiography. The War of the League of Cambrai is complex and its significance is sometimes overstated in narratives focused on Italian history. Montezuma II's rule is often portrayed as a period of Aztec decline, but modern scholarship suggests the empire remained powerful and stable in 1510; the decline came only with the arrival of Spanish conquistadors.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1510 is good for European events, particularly military and political history. Portuguese expansion in Asia is well-recorded in Portuguese sources but less well-documented in indigenous Asian sources. Chinese Ming records are comprehensive. The Aztec Empire is documented in both Spanish and indigenous sources, but these sources were compiled after the Spanish conquest and may reflect post-conquest biases. Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia (outside Portuguese contact zones) are largely absent from surviving written records.
